trigger.what_i_did: Created a Three.js scene with a WebGL renderer, perspective camera, coordinate transform, and render-loop scaffold for a 3D Sydney Harbour map.

card.lesson: Rendering is the moment your 3D map becomes something you can actually see on screen. You can think of the scene as a miniature harbour set: it has places, shapes, and a camera angle, but it is still sitting in the dark. The renderer is like a theatre lighting crew and photographer combined. It looks through the camera, works out what should be visible, then paints that view onto your screen. Each new view of the map needs to be rendered again.
card.question: What is the renderer mainly responsible for in your 3D map?
options: Turning the camera's view of the scene into the picture on your screen.
Choosing where the camera should sit before the scene is shown.
Storing the map's places so they stay there after you close it.
card.explanation: The renderer produces the visible screen image from the scene and camera. Camera placement is a separate choice, and saving map data is a separate storage job.

trigger.what_i_did: Added OrbitControls and camera limits so a user can pan, zoom, and orbit Sydney Harbour.

card.lesson: Camera position is where you stand when looking at the 3D harbour. Moving it changes what you can see, even though Sydney Harbour itself has not moved. It is like walking around a physical model on a table: step sideways to see behind a building, lean closer to inspect it, or step back for the whole view. Orbit controls let you move that viewing spot with your mouse or touch gestures. The camera limits are guardrails, like a rope around the model, stopping you from walking through it, getting impossibly close, or wandering so far away that the harbour disappears.
card.question: What do the camera limits mainly protect during navigation?
options: They keep your view within useful distances and angles around the harbour.
They make every harbour building stay permanently in the same place.
They redraw the harbour more often so it looks more detailed.
card.explanation: Camera limits constrain your viewpoint, not the harbour objects themselves or the scene's detail level.

trigger.what_i_did: Placed harbour landmarks from map coordinates into the 3D scene and added lighting for depth.

card.lesson: Coordinates are an address system for your 3D world. Just as a street address tells you where to deliver a parcel, a set of coordinates tells the scene where to place each harbour landmark. You give every landmark a position: left or right, forward or back, and up or down. That lets you turn information from a map into a scene that keeps the same spatial relationships: a lighthouse stays near the water, and a dock stays beside it. If an address is wrong, the parcel goes to the wrong house; if coordinates are wrong, the landmark appears in the wrong place.
card.question: What do coordinates mainly tell your 3D scene?
options: Where each landmark should appear in the scene.
How bright each landmark should look in the scene.
How quickly each landmark should move in the scene.
card.explanation: Coordinates are location addresses. Lighting controls brightness, while animation settings control movement.

trigger.what_i_did: Added pointer picking with Three.js Raycaster so selecting a landmark opens its details.

card.lesson: Perspective is the particular view your camera gives you of the 3D map: objects nearer to you look larger, and objects farther away look smaller. It also makes clicking work. When you point at a spot on the screen, the app imagines an invisible line starting at your viewpoint and passing through that spot. The first landmark that line reaches is the one selected. It is like shining a laser pointer from your eye through a window: whichever object the beam touches first is what you mean to point at.
card.question: When you click a landmark, how does the app decide what you meant to select?
options: It follows an invisible line from your viewpoint through the clicked screen spot.
It chooses the landmark whose label is closest to the clicked screen spot.
It selects whichever landmark was added to the map most recently.
card.explanation: The click becomes a line through the camera’s perspective, and the first landmark it reaches is selected. Labels and the order landmarks were added do not determine the result.

trigger.what_i_did: Finished the requestAnimationFrame update loop, responsive canvas resizing, and manual interaction checks.

card.lesson: An animation loop is the steady heartbeat that keeps your scene alive. Think of it like a flipbook in your hands: you show yourself a fresh picture again and again, so small changes become smooth movement. In your project, the loop repeatedly checks what has changed—such as a tap, drag, or moving object—and draws the newest version of the scene. Using the screen’s own timing helps those updates happen when the browser is ready to show them. Without this loop, the scene would behave more like a printed poster: it could appear once, but it would not keep responding and moving smoothly.
card.question: What is the animation loop mainly responsible for in your project?
options: It repeatedly refreshes the scene so changes and motion appear smoothly.
It stores the scene so it can reopen after the browser closes.
It stretches the scene so it always fills any screen size.
card.explanation: The loop keeps producing fresh frames, allowing movement and interaction to appear quickly and smoothly. Saving the scene and resizing the canvas are separate jobs.
